summary: Evans shares a framework for categorizing debugging skills, drawn from academic research on troubleshooting. She reframes five categories from a paper into actionable ways to improve: learn the codebase, learn the broader system, learn your tools, learn strategies, and get experience. Each category represents a different kind of knowledge needed for effective debugging, from specific codebase familiarity to general heuristics. She notes that experience is perhaps the most straightforward to acquire since programmers encounter bugs constantly throughout their careers.
key_points: Debugging skills can be categorized into five types: codebase knowledge, system/domain knowledge, tool knowledge, strategic knowledge, and experiential knowledge
Research suggests experts and novices use similar debugging strategies, but experts form more correct hypotheses due to experience
Debugging is itself one of the best ways to learn how a codebase works, creating a virtuous cycle
System knowledge (HTTP caching, CORS, database transactions) requires more intentional learning than codebase-specific knowledge
money_quotes: Debugging is also one of the best ways to learn how a new codebase works – seeing how something breaks helps you learn a lot about how it works.
Their findings did not show a significant difference in the strategies employed by the novices and experts. Experts simply formed more correct hypotheses and were more efficient at finding the fault.
I've had SO MANY bugs that were really frustrating and difficult the first time I ran into them, and very straightforward the fifth or tenth or 20th time.
All you need to do is investigate a million bugs, which is our whole life as programmers anyway.
I find that I often have to be a bit more intentional about learning systemic things like this – I need to actually take the time to look them up and read about them.
key_insight: Debugging expertise comes from five distinct types of knowledge — codebase, system, tools, strategies, and experience — and recognizing which you're missing helps you improve more deliberately.
